name: Tila
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: The most prestigious seed for Vata Hara (pacifies Vata dosha). Due to its Katu (pungent), Tikta (bitter), and Madhura (sweet) Rasa, Ushna (heating) Virya, and Madhura Vipaka, it is the premier choice for 'Snehana' (unctuous) and 'Vatavyadhi' (neurological disorders) therapies. While it is heating, its 'Madhura' Vipaka makes it uniquely nourishing for the 'Dhatus' (tissues).
Karma (Actions): Vatahara (Vata-pacifying), Balya (strength-promoter), Keshya (hair-tonic), Danta-harshahara (dental-tonic), Rasayana (rejuvenative), and Vrishya (aphrodisiac).
API/AFI Adherence: Known as 'Tila' in the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is the base for almost all classical medicated oils (Taila) and rejuvenative confections like Tiladi-gutika.
Specific Indications: Used in Vata-vyadhi (neuromuscular disorders), Kasha (Wasting), Khalitya (Hair loss), Danta-shoola (Toothache), and Asthibhanga (Fractures).
usageForm: Seeds (Tila), Medicated oil (Taila), Seed paste (Kalka), Roasted powder (Saktu).
mainMedicinalUses: World-Famous Vata and Neurological Specialist: Tila is the premier Ayurvedic seed for managing all chronic conditions of Vata imbalance (dryness, tremors, nerve pain). Its 'Snigdha' (oily) and 'Ushna' (heating) nature provided by sesamin and sesamolin helps in providing systemic lubrication and providing systemic stabilization of the nervous system. WHO research confirms its high content of lignans and heart-soothing properties.

Bone and Dental Health Specialist: Historically known as 'Dantya' (good for teeth). Its high calcium and magnesium content helps in strengthening the structural integrity of bones and teeth. It is used in 'Gandusha' (oil pulling) to manage tooth decay, sensitivity, and gum recession through its systemic nourishing property.

Hair Growth and Vitality (Keshya): One of the most prestigious herbs for hair health. The oil (Til-taila) penetrates deep into the scalp to stimulate hair follicles, prevent premature greying (Palitya), and manage alopecia (Khalitya) by providing systemic 'Raktaprasadana' (blood-soothing) effects.

Anti-Aging and Rejuvenation (Rasayana): Associated with 'Abhyanga' (oil massage). It helps in delaying age-related physical and mental decline and protecting the skin from oxidative stress through its potent antioxidant and immunity-enhancing profile.

Reproductive Health and Aphrodisiac (Vrishya): Traditionally used to improve sexual vigor and manage conditions like impotence by providing systemic nourishing (Brimhana) action and improving blood circulation in the pelvic region.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in Proteins (18-20%), Fats (Sesame oil), Lignans (Sesamin, Sesamolin), Flavonoids, and Essential Minerals (Calcium, Magnesium, Iron). Sesamin is the primary bioactive responsible for its potent anti-inflammatory and cardio-protective effects.
plantProfile: Unique History: Mentioned in the Vedas and Charaka Samhita as the best among 'Taila Dhanyas' (oily seeds). Historically used as a primary source of light and energy (Deepa-taila) and as a sacred offering (Til-anjali). Historically regarded as the 'Grain of Immortality'.

Physical Characteristics: A tall, erect, annual herb reaching up to 1-2 meters in height, with quadrangular stems and opposite, lanceolate leaves with white-to-pink funnel-shaped flowers. The fruit consists of small, inflated pods (capsules) containing numerous small, oval, black or white seeds. Found throughout India and tropical Asia, cultivated extensively for its seeds.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Contains Sesamin, Sesamolin, and Gamma-tocopherol. The presence of sesamin (approx. 0.5-1.0% in seeds) is crucial for its neuro-relaxant, anti-tumor, and anti-inflammatory action on the heart and brain cells.

Strict Standardizations: The seed is standardized in API based on its black or white color and characteristic sweet-mild-nutty taste. Physicochemical benchmarks: oil content (>40%), protein content (>18%).
preparationMethods: Til-Taila (Sesame Oil): Used as a base for almost all medicinal oils and for daily self-massage (Abhyanga).
Tila-Ladoo (Seed Sweets): 10-20g of seeds mixed with jaggery; taken for systemic strength and managing winter-related Vata issues.
Gandusha (Oil Pulling): 10-20ml of oil swished in the mouth for 10 minutes to manage dental health and jaw strength.
External Paste (Lepa): Ground fresh seeds or powder applied directly to localized inflammations (Mumps, boils, joint pain, skin rashes) to reduce heat and swelling.

dosages.Children: Generally safe. 5 - 10 grams of seeds mixed with jaggery once twice daily for muscle and bone development in childhood.
dosages.Adults: 10-20 grams of seeds or 10-30 ml of oil divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Highly recommended to prevent age-related bone density loss and joint stiffness. Dosage: 10g of seeds daily with warm milk.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Generally very safe. However, individuals with high Pitta (burning sensation, hyperacidity) should use it in moderation as it is Ushna (heating). Avoid during pregnancy in very large therapeutic doses except under professional guidance (as it may stimulate contractions).
GRAS Status: Classified as a safe staple food and oil source with a history of documented safety for thousands of years.
Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside medications as it significantly increases the bioavailability of many drugs. Can potentiate synthetic anticoagulants.
Adverse Effects: None documented at traditional therapeutic levels. Excessive use in v. sensitive individuals may cause mild skin warmth.
